an2.11:1.3: The *bala* of reflection and the *bala* of development [*bhāvanā*].
an2.11:1.4: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the *bala* of reflection?
an2.11:1.6: 'Bodily misconduct has a bad result, both in this very life and hereafter; verbal misconduct has a bad result, both in this very life and hereafter; mental misconduct has a bad result, both in this very life and hereafter.'
an2.11:1.7: Having so reflected, he abandons bodily misconduct and develops bodily good conduct; abandons verbal misconduct and develops verbal good conduct; abandons mental misconduct and develops mental good conduct; he maintains himself in purity.
an2.11:1.8: This, *bhikkhus*, is called the *bala* of reflection.
an2.11:2.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the *bala* of development [*bhāvanā*]?
an2.11:2.2: Now, *bhikkhus*, this *bala* of development [*bhāvanā*] is the *bala* of trainees.
an2.11:2.3: For relying on the trainee's *bala*, one abandons passion, abandons aversion, abandons delusion.
an2.11:2.4: Having abandoned passion, having abandoned aversion, having abandoned delusion, one does not do what is unwholesome, one does not engage in what is evil.
an2.11:2.5: This, *bhikkhus*, is called the *bala* of development [*bhāvanā*].
an2.12:1.3: The *bala* of reflection and the *bala* of development [*bhāvanā*].
an2.12:1.4: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the *bala* of reflection?
an2.12:1.6: 'Bodily misconduct has a bad result, both in this very life and hereafter; verbal misconduct has a bad result, both in this very life and hereafter; mental misconduct has a bad result, both in this very life and hereafter.'
an2.12:1.7: Having so reflected, he abandons bodily misconduct and develops bodily good conduct; abandons verbal misconduct and develops verbal good conduct; abandons mental misconduct and develops mental good conduct; he maintains himself in purity.
an2.12:1.8: This, *bhikkhus*, is called the *bala* of reflection.
an2.12:2.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the *bala* of development [*bhāvanā*]?
an2.12:2.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* develops the *sati-bojjhaṅga*, based on seclusion [*viveka*], based on dispassion, based on cessation, ripening in relinquishment;
an2.12:2.8: develops the *upekkhā-bojjhaṅga*, based on seclusion [*viveka*], based on dispassion, based on cessation, ripening in relinquishment.
an2.12:2.9: This, *bhikkhus*, is called the *bala* of development [*bhāvanā*].
an2.13:1.3: The *bala* of reflection and the *bala* of development [*bhāvanā*].
an2.13:1.4: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the *bala* of reflection?
an2.13:1.6: 'Bodily misconduct has a bad result, both in this very life and hereafter; verbal misconduct has a bad result, both in this very life and hereafter; mental misconduct has a bad result, both in this very life and hereafter.'
an2.13:1.7: Having so reflected, he abandons bodily misconduct and develops bodily good conduct; abandons verbal misconduct and develops verbal good conduct; abandons mental misconduct and develops mental good conduct; he maintains himself in purity.
an2.13:1.8: This, *bhikkhus*, is called the *bala* of reflection.
an2.13:2.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the *bala* of development [*bhāvanā*]?
an2.13:2.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unwholesome *dhammas*, with thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicara*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*viveka*], enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*.
an2.13:2.3: With the stilling of thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicara*], internally confident, with unification of mind [*citta*] [*cetaso ekodibhāvaṁ*], without thought [*vitakka*], without examination [*vicara*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of *samādhi*, enters and dwells in the second *jhāna*.
an2.13:2.4: With the fading of *pīti*, dwelling in *upekkhā*, with *sati* and clear comprehension [*sampajānā*], experiencing *sukha* with the body — what the noble ones describe as 'one with *upekkhā*, with *sati*, dwelling in *sukha*' — enters and dwells in the third *jhāna*.
an2.13:2.5: With the abandoning of *sukha* and *dukkha*, and with the prior passing away of joy and grief, neither-painful-nor-pleasant, with *upekkhā* and *sati* purified, enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*.
an2.13:2.6: This, *bhikkhus*, is called the *bala* of development [*bhāvanā*].
an2.14:1.1: "There are, *bhikkhus*, two ways the Tathāgata teaches *dhamma*.
an2.14:1.4: These, *bhikkhus*, are the two ways the Tathāgata teaches *dhamma*."
an2.15:1.1: In a case, *bhikkhus*, where neither the accused *bhikkhu* nor the accusing *bhikkhu* thoroughly examines himself by himself, it can be expected in that case
an2.15:1.2: that it will lead to prolongation, harshness, and severity, and the *bhikkhus* will not dwell at ease.
an2.15:1.3: But in a case, *bhikkhus*, where both the accused *bhikkhu* and the accusing *bhikkhu* thoroughly examine themselves by themselves, it can be expected in that case
an2.15:1.4: that it will not lead to prolongation, harshness, or severity, and the *bhikkhus* will dwell at ease.
an2.15:2.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, does the accused *bhikkhu* thoroughly examine himself by himself?
an2.15:2.10: Being displeased, I reported it to others.
an2.15:2.11: Thus the fault in this matter fell back entirely on me, like the customs duty on goods.'
an2.15:2.12: This, *bhikkhus*, is how the accused *bhikkhu* thoroughly examines himself by himself.
an2.15:2.3: 'I have indeed committed an unwholesome act in some bodily respect.
an2.15:2.4: That *bhikkhu* saw me committing an unwholesome act in some bodily respect.
an2.15:2.5: If I had not committed an unwholesome act in some bodily respect, that *bhikkhu* would not have seen me committing an unwholesome act in some bodily respect.
an2.15:2.6: But because I did commit an unwholesome act in some bodily respect, that *bhikkhu* saw me committing an unwholesome act in some bodily respect.
an2.15:2.7: And having seen me committing an unwholesome act in some bodily respect, that *bhikkhu* was displeased.
an2.15:2.8: Being displeased, that *bhikkhu* spoke words of displeasure to me.
an2.15:2.9: Having been spoken to with words of displeasure by that *bhikkhu*, I became displeased.
an2.15:3.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, does the accusing *bhikkhu* thoroughly examine himself by himself?
an2.15:3.10: Being displeased, he reported it to others.
an2.15:3.11: Thus the fault in this matter fell back entirely on me, like the customs duty on goods.'
an2.15:3.12: This, *bhikkhus*, is how the accusing *bhikkhu* thoroughly examines himself by himself.
an2.15:3.3: 'This *bhikkhu* has committed an unwholesome act in some bodily respect.
an2.15:3.4: I saw this *bhikkhu* committing an unwholesome act in some bodily respect.
an2.15:3.5: If this *bhikkhu* had not committed an unwholesome act in some bodily respect, I would not have seen this *bhikkhu* committing an unwholesome act in some bodily respect.
an2.15:3.6: But because this *bhikkhu* did commit an unwholesome act in some bodily respect, I saw this *bhikkhu* committing an unwholesome act in some bodily respect.
an2.15:3.7: And having seen this *bhikkhu* committing an unwholesome act in some bodily respect, I became displeased.
an2.15:3.8: Being displeased, I spoke words of displeasure to this *bhikkhu*.
an2.15:3.9: Having been spoken to with words of displeasure by me, this *bhikkhu* became displeased.
an2.15:4.1: In a case, *bhikkhus*, where neither the accused *bhikkhu* nor the accusing *bhikkhu* thoroughly examines himself by himself, it can be expected in that case that it will lead to prolongation, harshness, and severity, and the *bhikkhus* will not dwell at ease.
an2.15:4.2: But in a case, *bhikkhus*, where both the accused *bhikkhu* and the accusing *bhikkhu* thoroughly examine themselves by themselves, it can be expected in that case that it will not lead to prolongation, harshness, or severity, and the *bhikkhus* will dwell at ease."
an2.16:1.1: Then a certain brahmin approached the *Bhagavā* and exchanged greetings with him.
an2.16:1.2: Having exchanged courteous and amiable words, he sat down to one side. Seated to one side, that brahmin said this to the *Bhagavā*:
an2.16:1.3: "What, Master Gotama, is the cause, what is the condition, by which some beings here, with the breakup of the body after death, arise in a state of deprivation, a bad destination, a place of ruin, in hell?"
an2.16:1.4: "It is because of unrighteous and inequitable conduct, brahmin, that some beings here, with the breakup of the body after death, arise in a state of deprivation, a bad destination, a place of ruin, in hell."
an2.16:2.1: "What, Master Gotama, is the cause, what is the condition, by which some beings here, with the breakup of the body after death, arise in a good destination, a heavenly realm?"
an2.16:2.2: "It is because of righteous and equitable conduct, brahmin, that some beings here, with the breakup of the body after death, arise in a good destination, a heavenly realm."
an2.16:3.2: Just as if one were to set upright what had been overturned, or uncover what had been covered, or show the path to one who was lost, or hold up an oil lamp in the darkness so that those with eyes could see forms [*rūpa*] — even so has Master Gotama illuminated the *dhamma* in many ways.
an2.16:3.3: I go for refuge to Master Gotama, and to the *dhamma*, and to the *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus*.
an2.16:3.4: May Master Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge from this day forth for life."
an2.17:1.1: Then the brahmin Jāṇussoṇi approached the *Bhagavā* and exchanged greetings with him.
an2.17:1.10: It would be good if Master Gotama would teach *dhamma* in such a way that I might understand in full the meaning of what Master Gotama has said in brief and has not explained in full."
an2.17:1.11: "Then listen, brahmin, and attend carefully; I will speak."
an2.17:1.12: "Very well, sir," the brahmin Jāṇussoṇi replied to the *Bhagavā*.
an2.17:1.2: Having exchanged courteous and amiable words, he sat down to one side. Seated to one side, the brahmin Jāṇussoṇi said this to the *Bhagavā*:
an2.17:1.3: "What, Master Gotama, is the cause, what is the condition, by which some beings here, with the breakup of the body after death, arise in a state of deprivation, a bad destination, a place of ruin, in hell?"
an2.17:1.4: "Because of what has been done and what has not been done, brahmin.
an2.17:1.5: That is how some beings here, with the breakup of the body after death, arise in a state of deprivation, a bad destination, a place of ruin, in hell."
an2.17:1.6: "And what, Master Gotama, is the cause, what is the condition, by which some beings here, with the breakup of the body after death, arise in a good destination, a heavenly realm?"
an2.17:1.7: "Because of what has been done and what has not been done, brahmin.
an2.17:1.8: That is how some beings here, with the breakup of the body after death, arise in a good destination, a heavenly realm."
an2.17:1.9: "I do not understand in full the meaning of what Master Gotama has said in brief and has not explained in full.
an2.17:2.1: "Here, brahmin, in the case of one person, bodily misconduct has been done, and bodily good conduct has not been done;
an2.17:2.2: verbal misconduct has been done, and verbal good conduct has not been done;
an2.17:2.3: mental misconduct has been done, and mental good conduct has not been done.
an2.17:2.4: It is thus, brahmin, because of what has been done and what has not been done, that some beings here, with the breakup of the body after death, arise in a state of deprivation, a bad destination, a place of ruin, in hell.
an2.17:2.5: And here, brahmin, in the case of another person, bodily good conduct has been done, and bodily misconduct has not been done;
an2.17:2.6: verbal good conduct has been done, and verbal misconduct has not been done;
an2.17:2.7: mental good conduct has been done, and mental misconduct has not been done.
an2.17:2.8: It is thus, brahmin, because of what has been done and what has not been done, that some beings here, with the breakup of the body after death, arise in a good destination, a heavenly realm."
an2.17:3.1: "Excellent, Master Gotama! … May Master Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge from this day forth for life."
an2.18:1.1: Then the venerable Ānanda approached the *Bhagavā*, paid homage to him, and sat down to one side. To the venerable Ānanda seated to one side, the *Bhagavā* said this:
an2.18:1.10: As regards what I have declared without qualification to be things that should not be done — namely, bodily misconduct, verbal misconduct, and mental misconduct — when those things that should not be done are done, this danger is to be expected."
an2.18:1.2: "Without qualification, Ānanda, I declare bodily misconduct, verbal misconduct, and mental misconduct to be things that should not be done."
an2.18:1.3: "As regards what the *Bhagavā* has declared without qualification to be things that should not be done — namely, bodily misconduct, verbal misconduct, and mental misconduct — what danger is to be expected when those things that should not be done are done?"
an2.18:1.4: "As regards what I have declared without qualification to be things that should not be done — namely, bodily misconduct, verbal misconduct, and mental misconduct — when those things that should not be done are done, this danger is to be expected:
an2.18:1.6: the wise, on examination, find fault with one,
an2.18:1.9: with the breakup of the body after death, one arises in a state of deprivation, a bad destination, a place of ruin, in hell.
an2.18:2.1: "Without qualification, Ānanda, I declare bodily good conduct, verbal good conduct, and mental good conduct to be things that should be done."
an2.18:2.2: "As regards what the *Bhagavā* has declared without qualification to be things that should be done — namely, bodily good conduct, verbal good conduct, and mental good conduct — what benefit is to be expected when those things that should be done are done?"
an2.18:2.3: "As regards what I have declared without qualification to be things that should be done — namely, bodily good conduct, verbal good conduct, and mental good conduct — when those things that should be done are done, this benefit is to be expected:
an2.18:2.5: the wise, on examination, praise one,
an2.18:2.8: with the breakup of the body after death, one arises in a good destination, a heavenly realm.
an2.18:2.9: As regards what I have declared without qualification to be things that should be done — namely, bodily good conduct, verbal good conduct, and mental good conduct — when those things that should be done are done, this benefit is to be expected."
an2.19:1.2: It is possible, *bhikkhus*, to abandon what is unwholesome.
an2.19:1.3: If it were not possible, *bhikkhus*, to abandon what is unwholesome, I would not say:
an2.19:1.5: But because it is possible, *bhikkhus*, to abandon what is unwholesome, I say:
an2.19:1.7: If the abandoning of what is unwholesome were to lead to harm and *dukkha*, I would not say:
an2.19:1.9: But because the abandoning of what is unwholesome leads to welfare and *sukha*, I say:
an2.19:2.2: It is possible, *bhikkhus*, to develop what is wholesome.
an2.19:2.3: If it were not possible, *bhikkhus*, to develop what is wholesome, I would not say:
an2.19:2.5: But because it is possible, *bhikkhus*, to develop what is wholesome, I say:
an2.19:2.7: If the development of what is wholesome were to lead to harm and *dukkha*, I would not say:
an2.19:2.9: But because the development of what is wholesome leads to welfare and *sukha*, I say:
an2.20:1.1: "There are, *bhikkhus*, two *dhammas* that lead to the corruption and disappearance of the true *dhamma*.
an2.20:1.3: Wording that is poorly framed, and meaning that is poorly drawn out.
an2.20:1.4: When the wording is poorly framed, *bhikkhus*, the meaning too is difficult to draw out.
an2.20:1.5: These, *bhikkhus*, are the two *dhammas* that lead to the corruption and disappearance of the true *dhamma*."
an2.20:2.1: "There are, *bhikkhus*, two *dhammas* that lead to the persistence, non-corruption, and non-disappearance of the true *dhamma*.
an2.20:2.3: Wording that is well framed, and meaning that is well drawn out.
an2.20:2.4: When the wording is well framed, *bhikkhus*, the meaning too is easy to draw out.
an2.20:2.5: These, *bhikkhus*, are the two *dhammas* that lead to the persistence, non-corruption, and non-disappearance of the true *dhamma*."
an2.20:2.6: The second, the Legal Cases Chapter.
